title: What to Do the Day You Activate a Power of Attorney
summary: Activating a POA — actually starting to use it — often happens suddenly, in the middle of a crisis. Here's what the first steps typically look like.
category: Life Transitions III: Legal & Financial Shifts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: What to Do the Day You Activate a Power of Attorney | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Activating a POA — actually starting to use it — often happens suddenly, in the middle of a crisis. Here's what the first steps typically look like.
whatYouNeedToKnow: You'll likely need the original (or certified copy) of the POA document, plus ID.
Financial institutions each have their own internal process for recognizing a POA.
Starting with the most urgent accounts (checking, bills) first is usually the practical priority.

paragraphs: Most institutions require the original or a certified copy of the POA document along with your own identification before they'll recognize your authority — it's worth confirming this requirement and having documents ready before you actually need to use them.

Each bank or institution has its own internal verification process, which can take longer than expected — starting with the accounts tied to the most urgent, time-sensitive needs (like an upcoming bill or medical payment) is usually the practical place to begin.

nextSteps: Locate the original POA document and confirm where certified copies can be obtained if needed.
Contact the most urgent accounts first.
Ask each institution directly what their specific POA process requires.
disclaimer: This article is general information, not legal advice, and laws vary by state. The specific requirements, deadlines, and procedures described here may not apply exactly to your situation. Confirm all details with a licensed elder law attorney before acting, especially before signing, filing, or relying on any document.

sources.name: What is a power of attorney (POA)?
